Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Feb-02-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Amy Todd-Gher, Esq. of Littler Mendelson PC, T:619.515.1872, for defendant/moving party SQUARETRADE, INC.;
John S. Hong, Esq. of Littler Mendelson PC, T:415.677.4080, for defendant/joining party ALLSTATEN INSURANCE COMPANY, INC.;
Aaron Minnis, Esq. of Minnis & Smallets, T:415.551.0885, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT SQUARETRADE, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings as follows:

Defendants' motions to compel arbitration are GRANTED. Plaintiff has two arguments, both unavailing.

First, Plaintiff contends her claims are governed not by the arbitration provision of her employment contract, but rather by a "California Employee Proprietary Information and Inventions Agreement" (PIIA) signed later. However, the PIIA applies only to a relatively narrow class of intellectual property claims that could be filed "against" Plaintiff, and no claims were filed against Plaintiff. Moreover, the relevant clause is a "consent to personal jurisdiction," not an agreement to adjudicate any particular claim in any forum. To the extent Plaintiff argues arbitration agreements must be bilateral, only a "modicum of bilaterality" is required, and that is present here. (See Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 117.)

Second, Plaintiff argues that the arbitration agreement is unconscionable. However, Plaintiff is a sophisticated business person who had adequate time to read the plain-English arbitration agreement and does not claim she failed to understand it. The clauses Plaintiff highlights as supposedly improper are inapplicable here and/or severable, and the agreement meets all of the California Supreme Court's basic requirements. (Id. at 102, 117.)

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: torreanom@aol.com; mtorreano@sftc.org.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Join Defendant Squaretrae Incs Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay All Proceedings
Off Calendar Feb-02-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Amy Todd-Gher, Esq. of Littler Mendelson PC, T:619.515.1872, for defendant/moving party SQUARETRADE, INC.;
John S. Hong, Esq. of Littler Mendelson PC, T:415.677.4080, for defendant/joining party ALLSTATEN INSURANCE COMPANY, INC.;
Aaron Minnis, Esq. of Minnis & Smallets, T:415.551.0885, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT ALLSTATE INSURANCE COMPANY, INC.'s Motion To Join Defendant Squaretrade Inc.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay All Proceedings.

Defendants' motions to compel arbitration are GRANTED. Plaintiff has two arguments, both unavailing.

First, Plaintiff contends her claims are governed not by the arbitration provision of her employment contract, but rather by a "California Employee Proprietary Information and Inventions Agreement" (PIIA) signed later. However, the PIIA applies only to a relatively narrow class of intellectual property claims that could be filed "against" Plaintiff, and no claims were filed against Plaintiff. Moreover, the relevant clause is a "consent to personal jurisdiction," not an agreement to adjudicate any particular claim in any forum. To the extent Plaintiff argues arbitration agreements must be bilateral, only a "modicum of bilaterality" is required, and that is present here. (See Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 117.)

Second, Plaintiff argues that the arbitration agreement is unconscionable. However, Plaintiff is a sophisticated business person who had adequate time to read the plain-English arbitration agreement and does not claim she failed to understand it. The clauses Plaintiff highlights as supposedly improper are inapplicable here and/or severable, and the agreement meets all of the California Supreme Court's basic requirements. (Id. at 102, 117.)

Prevailing party to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the Court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: torreanom@aol.com; mtorreano@sftc.org.
